name: Essential Chinese
description: Your first steps in Mandarin: the complete 150-word HSK 1 vocabulary plus the survival phrases you need on day one, from greetings to haggling. Simplified characters on the front; pinyin and meaning on the back.

fields.extra: The universal greeting. 你 (you) + 好 (good). Two 3rd tones in a row: the first shifts to 2nd tone, so it sounds like ní hǎo.

fields.back: nǐ hǎo ma - how are you?
fields.extra: 吗 turns a statement into a yes-no question. Standard reply: 我很好 (I'm fine).
很 is required glue between a subject and an adjective; bare 我好 sounds wrong. Here it barely means 'very'.
The second syllable is a light neutral tone. Add the person: 谢谢你.
Literally 'don't be polite'. 不 shifts to 2nd tone before a 4th tone, so you say bú kèqi.
The apology for real faults: bumping into someone, being late. The middle 不 is a neutral tone here.

fields.back: méi guānxi - it doesn't matter, no problem
fields.extra: The standard reply to 对不起. Literally 'there is no connection'.
再 (again) + 见 (see): 'see you again'.
Starts polite requests: 请坐 (please sit), 请喝茶 (please have tea). Also 'to treat': 我请你 (my treat).

fields.back: qǐngwèn - excuse me, may I ask...
fields.extra: The polite opener before any question to a stranger: 请问，厕所在哪儿？

fields.back: nǐ jiào shénme míngzi - what is your name?
fields.extra: 叫 (to be called) + 什么 (what) + 名字 (name). Answer with 我叫 plus your name.

fields.back: wǒ bù dǒng - I don't understand
fields.extra: 懂 (to understand). For spoken language you will also hear 我听不懂 (I can hear it but not understand it).

fields.back: qǐng zài shuō yī biàn - please say it again
fields.extra: 再 (again) + 说 (say) + 一遍 (one time through). 一 shifts to 2nd tone before a 4th tone: yí biàn.

fields.back: qǐng shuō màn yīdiǎn - please speak more slowly
fields.extra: Adjective + 一点 means 'a bit more so'. 一 is pronounced yì before a 3rd tone: yìdiǎn.

fields.back: nǐ huì shuō Yīngyǔ ma - do you speak English?
fields.extra: 会 marks a learned ability. 英语 (English language); swap in 汉语 to ask about Chinese.

fields.back: zhè shì shénme - what is this?
fields.extra: Point and ask. Chinese question words stay where the answer would go; nothing moves to the front like English 'what'.

fields.back: duōshao qián - how much is it?
fields.extra: The essential shopping question: 多少 (how much) + 钱 (money). No verb needed: 这个多少钱？
太...了 wraps an adjective to mean 'too/so'. 贵 (expensive). Your opening move in any market haggle.

fields.back: wǒ yào zhège - I want this one
fields.extra: 要 (to want) + 这个 (this one). Point at the menu and say it; works in any shop or restaurant.

fields.back: wǒ bù yào - I don't want it, no thanks
fields.extra: Firm but not rude; perfect for street vendors. Pronounced wǒ bú yào (不 shifts to 2nd tone before a 4th tone).
块 is the spoken word for the yuan (the formal word is 元 yuán). Prices are number + 块, and 钱 often drops off: 十块.

fields.back: cèsuǒ zài nǎr - where is the toilet?
fields.extra: 厕所 (toilet) + 在 (to be at) + 哪儿 (where). The pattern for finding anything: X 在哪儿？
Call this out to get attention in a restaurant; it is normal, not rude. Literally 'service person'.
Say it to the server when you want to pay. Originally Cantonese; now universal in mainland restaurants.
Literally 'dry the cup'. At formal toasts it can mean bottoms-up, so pace yourself.
等 (to wait) + 一下 (a little). 一 is pronounced yí before the 4th tone: yíxià.
问题 (problem, question). The easygoing yes to any request.
救 (to save) + 命 (life). Only for real emergencies; for everyday help say 请帮我 (please help me).
Add 的 for 'my': 我的书 (my book).
A polite form 您 nín exists, but 你 is fine in nearly every situation.
他 (he), 她 (she) and 它 (it) all sound identical: tā. Only the writing differs. The person radical 亻 sits on the left.
Same sound as 他 (he); the woman radical 女 marks 'she' in writing only.
们 makes pronouns plural: 我们 (we), 你们 (you all), 他们 (they).
Pairs with a measure word before nouns: 这个人 (this person), 这本书 (this book).
Mirror of 这 (this): 那个人 (that person). Don't confuse it with 哪 nǎ (which), which has an extra 口.
Fills empty slots when reading numbers aloud: room 302 reads sān líng èr.
Tone changes in speech: yí before 4th tones (一个 yí gè), yì before other tones (一本 yì běn); plain yī when counting.
For counting and math. Before measure words use 两 liǎng instead: 两个人 (two people), never 二个人.
三点 (3 o'clock), 三个人 (three people).
Considered unlucky (it sounds like 死 sǐ, death); buildings often skip the 4th floor. Mind sì (4) vs shí (10).
五块钱 (five yuan).
A lucky number; 666 in Chinese chat means 'awesome', nothing sinister.
七点 (7 o'clock).
The lucky number: it sounds like 发 fā (to get rich). Phone numbers and plates full of 8s cost extra.
Sounds like 久 jiǔ (long-lasting), so nine is auspicious for anniversaries.
Numbers 11 to 99 just stack: 十一 (11), 二十 (20), 二十五 (25). Mind sì (4) vs shí (10).
一百 (100), 三百 (300). 105 is 一百零五, with 零 filling the empty tens slot.
天 (day) also builds 明天 (tomorrow) and 昨天 (yesterday); learn the trio together.
明 (bright: sun 日 + moon 月) + 天 (day): the next bright day. 明天见 (see you tomorrow).
昨 carries the sun radical 日: a day already passed.
上 (upper) + 午 (noon). The day splits at noon: 上午, 中午, 下午.
中 (middle) + 午 (noon), the same 中 as in 中国 (China, the Middle Kingdom).
下 (lower) + 午 (noon): the part of the day below noon.
现在几点？ (What time is it now?)
三点 (3 o'clock). Also 'a little' in 一点儿 and 'to order food' in 点菜.
For durations: 五分钟 (five minutes). Clock-time minutes drop 钟: 三点十分 (3:10).
Dates run big to small: 2026年7月9日. 新年 (New Year).
Months are just numbers: 一月 (January) through 十二月 (December). The character draws a crescent moon.

fields.back: hào - day of the month; number
fields.extra: 几号？ (What's the date?) 五号 (the 5th). Also numbers of rooms and buses: 八号 (No. 8).
Weekdays count up: 星期一 (Monday) to 星期六 (Saturday); Sunday is 星期天 or 星期日.
什么时候？ (When?). ...的时候 means 'when/while': 吃饭的时候 (while eating).
Links nouns only: 我是学生 (I am a student). Never use it before adjectives; say 我很好, not 我是好.
Negated with 没, never 不: 没有 (not have). 有问题吗？ (Any questions?)

fields.back: méiyǒu - to not have; did not
fields.extra: Also negates completed actions: 我没有去 (I did not go). Often shortened to just 没.
去 + place: 去中国 (go to China), 去学校 (go to school).
Also 'bring me' when ordering: 来一杯茶 (a cup of tea, please).
回家 (go home), 回国 (return to your country). The character is a spiral turning back on itself.
看书 (read a book), 看电视 (watch TV), 看朋友 (visit a friend). A hand 手 shading an eye 目.

fields.back: kànjiàn - to see, catch sight of
fields.extra: 看 is the act of looking; 看见 is the result: 我看了，没看见 (I looked but didn't see it).
听不懂 (hear but not understand) is the phrase you will use most in week one.
说汉语 (speak Chinese). 说话 means to talk in general.
读书 (read, study). The classroom sense is reading aloud.
写字 (write characters), 写名字 (write your name).
吃饭 (have a meal, literally 'eat rice'). 你吃了吗？ (Have you eaten?) doubles as a friendly greeting.
喝水 (drink water), 喝茶 (drink tea). The mouth radical 口 marks a mouth action; compare 和 hé (and), same right half.
做饭 (cook). 你做什么工作？ (What work do you do?). Same sound as 坐 (to sit); context decides.
买东西 (buy things, go shopping). Its opposite 卖 mài (to sell) differs only by tone and a top stroke: 3rd tone buys, 4th tone sells.

fields.back: kāi - to open; to start; to drive
fields.extra: 开门 (open the door), 开车 (drive a car).
请坐 (please sit). 坐飞机 (take a plane), 坐出租车 (take a taxi). Two people 人 sitting on earth 土.
你住在哪儿？ (Where do you live?). Also for hotels: 住饭店 (stay at a hotel).

fields.back: jiào - to be called; to call
fields.extra: 我叫 plus your name (My name is...). Also to shout or summon: 叫出租车 (call a taxi).
我学习汉语 (I study Chinese). 学 alone works too: 学汉语.
Both verb and noun: 我在中国工作 (I work in China); 你的工作 (your job).
睡 (to sleep) + 觉 (a sleep). 我要睡觉 (I want to sleep). Don't confuse with 水饺 shuǐjiǎo (boiled dumplings); tones matter.

fields.back: dǎ diànhuà - to make a phone call
fields.extra: 打 (to hit) is the verb for phone calls: 给我打电话 (call me). 电话 is literally 'electric speech'.
Literally 'rain falls down'. 今天下雨 (it's raining today). 雨 is a picture of raindrops falling from a sky.
我爱你 (I love you). Strong; for everyday liking use 喜欢.
我喜欢中国菜 (I like Chinese food). Takes nouns or whole verbs: 喜欢看电影 (like watching movies).

fields.back: xiǎng - to want to; to think; to miss
fields.extra: 想 + verb means want to: 我想去 (I want to go). With a person it means to miss: 我想你.

fields.back: rènshi - to know (a person); to recognize
fields.extra: 认识你很高兴 (Nice to meet you). For knowing facts, Chinese uses a different verb (知道 zhīdào, HSK 2).
Learned abilities: 我会说汉语 (I can speak Chinese). Compare 能 néng for in-the-moment ability.

fields.back: néng - can, to be able to
fields.extra: Ability or permission in the moment: 我不能去 (I can't go). Skills you learned take 会 instead.
Location: 我在家 (I am at home). Before a verb it marks an ongoing action: 我在吃饭 (I am eating).
Second syllable is neutral tone. Casual and universal; adults use it too.
Woman radical 女 plus 马 mǎ (horse) lending the sound. 妈/马/吗 (mā/mǎ/ma) is the classic first tone drill.
子 here is a neutral-tone noun suffix, the same one as in 杯子, 桌子, 椅子.
女 (female) + 儿 (child). The apostrophe separates the syllables in pinyin, and ü keeps its dots after n.
老 (old, venerable) + 师 (master). Address teachers as surname + 老师: 王老师.
学 (to study) + 生 (person). 我是学生 (I am a student).
同 (same) + 学 (study): someone you study with. Teachers address a class as 同学们.
男朋友 (boyfriend) and 女朋友 (girlfriend) add 男 (male) or 女 (female) in front, so mind the difference.
医 (medicine) + 生 (person). 医院 (hospital) shares the same 医.
Surname first: 王先生 (Mr. Wang). Literally 'born earlier'.
Fine after a surname: 王小姐 (Miss Wang). Calling a stranger 小姐 by itself can offend in the mainland; say 服务员 to staff instead.
热水 (hot water) is the default drink offered in China. 水果 (fruit) starts with the same character.
喝茶 (drink tea). The word traveled the world: both 'chai' and 'cha' come from this character.
米 (raw rice) + 饭 (cooked food, meal). 吃饭 (have a meal) uses the same 饭.
中国菜 (Chinese food), 点菜 (order dishes). The grass radical 艹 on top hints at its vegetable origin.
水 (water) + 果 (fruit): juicy produce. 果 appears again in 苹果 (apple).
Also the brand: 苹果手机 (iPhone).
一杯茶 (a cup of tea): 杯 doubles as the measure word for drinks. Same 杯 as in 干杯 (cheers).
A pig 豕 under a roof 宀: the ancient picture of a household. 回家 (go home).
学 (study) + 校 (school building). 去学校 (go to school).
饭 (meal) + 店 (shop). In big cities a 饭店 is often a hotel; a small eatery is a 饭馆.
商 (commerce) + 店 (shop). 去商店买东西 (go to the store to buy things).
医 (medicine) + 院 (institution). 去医院 (go to the hospital).
中 (middle) + 国 (country): the Middle Kingdom. 中国人 (Chinese person), 中国菜 (Chinese food).
北 (north) + 京 (capital): the northern capital. 南京 Nánjīng is the southern one.
桌子上 (on the table). Also 'last': 上个星期 (last week). Pairs with 下 in both senses.
桌子下 (under the table); 下个月 (next month). The stroke points below the line; in 上 it points above.
饭店在前面 (the restaurant is up ahead). 面 (side, face) turns 前 into a place word.
在学校后面 (behind the school). Opposite of 前面.
Follows the noun: 家里 (at home), 学校里 (inside the school).
A picture of a walking person. Country + 人 gives nationality: 中国人 (Chinese), 美国人 (American).
你叫什么名字？ (What is your name?). Chinese names put the family name first.
多少钱？ (How much?). The metal radical 钅 recalls coins.
看书 (read), 一本书 (one book, with measure word 本).
汉 (the Han people) + 语 (language). You will also hear 中文 Zhōngwén; both are fine.
汉字 (Chinese characters): a child 子 under a roof 宀. 名字 (name) uses it too.
Wood radical 木 at the bottom of 桌. The suffix 子 builds everyday-object nouns: 桌子, 椅子, 杯子.
Wood radical 木 again, this time on the left. 坐在椅子上 (sit on the chair).
买衣服 (buy clothes). 衣 alone is the clothing radical you will meet inside other characters.
Literally 'east-west'. 买东西 (buy things, shop). With a neutral xi it means stuff; with full tones it means the compass directions.
天 (sky) + 气 (air). 今天天气怎么样？ (How is the weather today?)
Animal radical 犭 on the left. Onomatopoeic: māo sounds like meow.
Same animal radical 犭 as 猫 (cat). 小狗 (puppy).
飞 (to fly) + 机 (machine). 坐飞机 (take a plane); the airport, 机场, shares the 机.
出租 (to rent out) + 车 (vehicle): a car for hire. 坐出租车 (take a taxi).
电 (electric) + 视 (vision). 看电视 (watch TV). 电 starts a whole family: 电脑, 电影, 电话.
Literally 'electric brain': 电 (electric) + 脑 (brain).
Literally 'electric shadow'. 看电影 (see a movie); a cinema is 电影院.
Woman 女 + child 子 = good. Also an agreeable 'OK' on its own: 好！or 好的.
A person with arms stretched wide. 大学 (university, literally 'big school').
小狗 (puppy), 小姐 (Miss). 小 + surname is a friendly address for someone younger: 小王.
很多 (a lot). 多少 (how many) literally stacks 'many-few'.
Opposite of 多. Read shào it means young, but that reading is beyond HSK 1.
今天很冷 (it's cold today). The two-stroke ice radical 冫 sits on the left.
热水 (hot water), 天气很热 (the weather is hot). The four dots at the bottom are the fire radical.
认识你很高兴 (nice to meet you). 高 (high) + 兴 (spirits).
The liang is a neutral tone. Works for people, clothes, and cities alike: 很漂亮.
The all-purpose negator: 不是, 不去, 不好. Shifts to bú before 4th tones: 不是 bú shì. Use 没 to negate 有.
Default glue between subject and adjective: 他很高兴 (he is happy). Often so weak it just means 'is'.
The 太...了 pattern: 太贵了 (too expensive), 太好了 (great!).
Goes after the subject, before the verb: 我们都去 (we are all going).
Stays where the answer would go: 这是什么？ (This is what?). 为什么 (why) builds on it.
Also read shuí in formal speech; shéi is what you will hear. 他是谁？ (Who is he?)
哪 + measure word: 哪个 (which one), 你是哪国人？ (What nationality are you?). It is 那 (that) plus a mouth 口 asking the question.
你在哪儿？ (Where are you?). Southern speakers and Taiwan say 哪里 nǎlǐ instead.
For answers under about ten, always with a measure word: 几个人？ (How many people?). 几点？ (What time?)
For larger or unknown quantities, no measure word needed: 多少钱？ (How much money?)
怎么说？ (How do you say it?), 怎么去？ (How do I get there?)

fields.back: zěnmeyàng - how is it? how about it?
fields.extra: The all-purpose 'what do you think': 天气怎么样？ (How's the weather?), 这个怎么样？ (How about this one?)
Statement + 吗 = question: 你是老师吗？ (Are you a teacher?). Neutral tone, mouth radical 口.
Returns a question: 我很好，你呢？ (I'm fine, and you?)
The default classifier: 一个人, 三个苹果. Number + measure word + noun is mandatory in Chinese. Often neutral in speech: 一个 yí ge.
一本书 (one book). A tree 木 with a mark at its base; the original meaning was 'root'.

fields.back: kuài - (measure word for money; piece, lump)
fields.extra: 十块钱 (ten yuan). Also chunks of things: 一块 (a piece).
Age needs no verb: 我三十岁 (I am 30). 你几岁？ works for children; adults get 你多大？
这些 (these), 那些 (those), 一些 (some). The plural-ish counterpart of 个.
Pronounced yìdiǎnr. 我会说一点儿汉语 (I speak a little Chinese). After adjectives: 便宜一点儿 (a bit cheaper).
我的书 (my book), 你的名字 (your name). The most common character in Chinese; always neutral tone.
After a verb for completed actions: 我买了 (I bought it). At sentence end for new situations: 下雨了 (it's raining now).
Joins nouns only: 我和你 (you and I). It cannot join sentences the way English 'and' does.
Answering the phone it rises to 2nd tone: wéi. Elsewhere it is a blunt 'hey' to get attention.
